blocks.statement: I can construct a ray diagram for a plane mirror, locate the virtual image at equal distance behind the mirror, and explain why the image is upright + same size.

blocks.whatWeKnow: Radio telescopes are giant CURVED mirrors that focus radio waves to a detector. The Day 4 reflection law applies directly.
blocks.connection: Today the FLAT-mirror case. Day 14 generalizes to curves (lenses). Day 15 mentions parabolic radio dishes that combine both ideas.

blocks.markdown:
## Where IS your mirror image, really?

When you look in a flat mirror, your brain does ray-tracing without asking permission. Light leaves your face, reflects off the mirror obeying the Day 4 law (θᵢ = θᵣ), and enters your eye. Your brain assumes light travels in straight lines, so it traces those rays **backward** — and they converge at a point **behind** the mirror.

No light is actually there. That's a **virtual image**: it can't be caught on a screen, because the rays never really meet. But the geometry is exact and predictable:

- **d_image = d_object** — the image sits exactly as far behind the mirror as you stand in front of it.
- **h_image = h_object** — same size, no magnification.
- **Upright** — a plane mirror never flips you top-to-bottom.

Two rays from any point on the object, reflected by the law and traced backward, are enough to locate the image. That two-ray construction is today's core skill — and it's the same construction that explains why a radio dish needs to be CURVED to focus signals to one point.

terms.definition: A flat reflecting surface. Light obeys the law of reflection (Day 4). Produces VIRTUAL images behind the mirror at equal distance from the object. Example: your bathroom mirror, a glass storefront, a still pond surface. "Plane" means flat — not like an airplane; curved mirrors (concave, convex) behave differently and are not on the CPA scope.
terms.cognate: Sp. espejo plano · Pt. espelho plano · HC miwa plat

terms.definition: An image that LIGHT DOES NOT ACTUALLY REACH. The brain TRACES light rays backward and locates the image at the convergence point. Cannot be projected on a screen. Example: your mirror image — the light reflects off the mirror and into your eye; the image APPEARS to be behind the mirror, but no light is actually there.
terms.cognate: Sp. imagen virtual · Pt. imagem virtual · HC imaj vityèl

blocks.title: Watch out
blocks.markdown: A virtual image cannot be 'caught' on a screen. A real image CAN. Plane mirrors always make virtual images.

blocks.instruction: Object on the left of the mirror. Draw two rays from the object's tip — through the law of reflection. Trace each reflected ray BACKWARD (dashed). They converge at the VIRTUAL image, equal distance behind the mirror.
prompts: Draw the mirror as a vertical line and the object on the left.
Reflect two rays from the object's tip using θᵢ = θᵣ.
Extend the reflected rays backward as dashed lines and mark where they converge — the virtual image.

blocks.prompt: IMAGE LOCATION. A 2.0-m-tall person stands 1.5 m from a flat mirror. Where is their image? How tall does it appear?
blocks.given: height = 2.0 m · d_object = 1.5 m from mirror
blocks.work:
d_image = 1.5 m behind the mirror.
h_image = 2.0 m tall.
Image is upright + virtual + same size.
blocks.answer: 1.5 m behind the mirror, 2.0 m tall, upright.

blocks.prompt: CLOSING THE DISTANCE. You walk 0.5 m closer to a mirror. By how much do you and your image close on each other?
blocks.given: You move 0.5 m toward mirror. Mirror is stationary.
blocks.equation: d_you = − 0.5 m → d_image = + 0.5 m closer also (mirror)
blocks.work:
Your distance to mirror: ↓ 0.5 m
Image's distance to mirror (= your distance): ↓ 0.5 m
Image moves 0.5 m TOWARD mirror also.
Total closing: 1.0 m.
blocks.answer: You and your image close on each other by 1.0 m total.

blocks.prompt: YOUR TURN — SHORTEST MIRROR TO SEE YOURSELF. What's the shortest mirror that lets you see your full body (top of head to feet)? Hint: trace two rays — one from your eyes to your feet, one from your eyes to the top of your head. The mirror only needs to cover the midpoints.
blocks.givenHint: Your full height h. Eyes near the top of your head. Mirror is vertical, flat.
blocks.equationHint: Trace the eye-to-feet ray and the eye-to-head-top ray. Each reflection point sits at the MIDPOINT — so how much mirror do you actually need?

blocks.title: The answer to the YOUR TURN problem
blocks.markdown: A mirror HALF your height, mounted at the right height (top edge at eye level + half of forehead, bottom edge at feet midpoint), shows you head-to-toe regardless of how far away you stand.

blocks.prompt: A 2-m-tall person stands 1.5 m from a flat mirror. Where is their image? How tall does it appear? Answer in one line.
blocks.frame: A plane mirror produces a ___ image (light doesn't actually reach it). The image is the same ___ as the object, located the same ___ behind the mirror as the object is in front. It is ___ (upright/inverted).

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **27.5–27.8** (opaque materials, shadows and eclipses, polarization, and 3-D viewing). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
